chapter_summary: The Isha Upanishad, with Shankara's commentary, presents the nature of the Self (Atman) as pure, non-dual, and all-pervading. It distinguishes between the path of knowledge (jnana) for renunciates and the path of action (karma) for householders, ultimately advocating for the unity of the Self in all beings. The text includes mantras for meditation on the sun as a symbol of truth and a prayer for guidance.

story.title: Isha Upanishad
story.arc: Exposition of the nature of the Self and the paths to liberation
story.timeline_note: Timeless; the Upanishad is a philosophical text without narrative chronology.
story.comic_potential: Low; the text is a philosophical discourse with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: The entire universe is to be enveloped by the Lord; renounce and enjoy; do not covet anyone's wealth.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the practice of inner renunciation and detachment from material desires, aligning with the principle of 'tyaga' (letting go) as a foundation for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: One should desire to live a hundred years performing actions; thus no sin clings to a person.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the path of 'karma-yoga' where actions are performed without attachment, purifying the mind and preparing it for higher knowledge.

teachings.teaching: Those who slay the Self go to dark worlds covered with blinding darkness.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against ignorance of the Self; in Kriya-Yoga, it underscores the importance of self-awareness and the danger of identifying solely with the body and mind.

teachings.teaching: The Self is unmoving, one, swifter than the mind; the senses cannot reach it; it stands still and outruns all runners.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as describing the Atman as the silent witness, the unchanging consciousness that underlies all mental and physical activity.

teachings.teaching: It moves and does not move; it is far and near; it is within all and outside all.
teachings.lens_note: This paradox points to the non-dual nature of reality; in Kriya-Yoga, it is realized through meditation that transcends spatial and temporal distinctions.

teachings.teaching: One who sees all beings in the Self and the Self in all beings never hates.
teachings.lens_note: This is the culmination of Kriya-Yoga practice, where the practitioner experiences unity with all existence, leading to universal love and compassion.

teachings.teaching: When one realizes all beings become the Self, what delusion and sorrow can there be?
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that this realization eradicates all suffering, as the sense of separation is dissolved.

teachings.teaching: The Self is all-pervading, luminous, bodiless, without wound, without sinews, pure, untouched by evil; the seer, the thinker, the all-pervading, self-existent; it duly allotted duties to the eternal Prajapatis.
teachings.lens_note: This describes the nature of the Self as pure consciousness; in Kriya-Yoga, it is the goal of meditation to realize this innate purity.

teachings.teaching: Those who worship ignorance enter blinding darkness; those who delight in knowledge enter even greater darkness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as a warning against one-sided approaches; both action without knowledge and knowledge without action are incomplete.

teachings.teaching: One thing is said to result from knowledge, another from ignorance; thus we have heard from the wise.
teachings.lens_note: This acknowledges the different fruits of the two paths; in Kriya-Yoga, it suggests that both have their place in spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: One who knows both knowledge and ignorance together, crosses death through ignorance and attains immortality through knowledge.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the integration of action and knowledge, where karma-yoga purifies the mind and jnana-yoga leads to liberation.

teachings.teaching: Those who worship the unmanifest enter blinding darkness; those who delight in the manifest enter even greater darkness.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against exclusive worship of either the unmanifest cause or the manifest effect; in Kriya-Yoga, it points to the need for balanced practice.

teachings.teaching: One thing is said to result from the manifest, another from the unmanifest; thus we have heard from the wise.
teachings.lens_note: Similar to verse 10, this acknowledges different fruits; in Kriya-Yoga, it encourages the aspirant to understand both aspects.

teachings.teaching: One who knows both the manifest and the unmanifest together, crosses death through the unmanifest and attains immortality through the manifest.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the synthesis of meditation on the formless and the formed, leading to complete liberation.

teachings.teaching: The face of truth is covered by a golden vessel; O Pusan, uncover it for the sake of truth, for me who am devoted to truth.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is a prayer for the removal of ignorance, allowing the inner light of the Self to shine forth.

teachings.teaching: O Pusan, sole seer, Yama, Surya, son of Prajapati, withdraw your rays; I see your most beautiful form; that Purusha who is there, I am He.
teachings.lens_note: This mantra affirms the identity of the individual self with the cosmic Self; in Kriya-Yoga, it is a realization of non-duality.

teachings.teaching: May my life-breath go to the immortal wind; may this body end in ashes; O mind, remember, remember your deeds.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as a preparation for death, focusing on the eternal nature of the Self and the transience of the body.

teachings.teaching: O Agni, lead us by the good path to prosperity; remove our crooked sin; we shall offer many praises to you.
teachings.lens_note: This is a prayer for guidance and purification; in Kriya-Yoga, it symbolizes the inner fire of discipline that burns away impurities.
